The SWS DCF model estimates what a business might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today. That process focuses on cash generation potential rather than current revenue or earnings, which is relevant for a late stage biopharma name with phase 3 programs and limited current revenue.

Pharvaris is currently loss making, reports less than $1m in revenue and is forecast to remain unprofitable over the next three years, so most of the modeled value sits in expected future cash flows if its pipeline succeeds. Against that, all liabilities come from higher risk external funding rather than customer deposits, and recent shareholder dilution means existing holders have already absorbed some of the funding cost of that growth path.

Investors weighing that very large modeled intrinsic discount also have to contend with a P/B of 5.9x that screens as expensive versus both direct peers and the broader industry, along with an analyst price target that is more than 20% above the current share price but comes with limited agreement between forecasters. Together, those signals point to a wide spread between what different models and market participants currently assume about Pharvaris's long term cash generation, and underline why understanding the SWS DCF model inputs can be useful before leaning too hard on any single fair value line in the sand.
